RE: Informal Assistance

Dear Us. Donovan:
This firm represents the California Council of Civil Engineers
& Land Surveyors, a professional association of civil
engineering and land surveying firns located throughout the
State of California. 
As you knorr, we previously requested
and received your office,s infornal assistance regarding File
No. I-89-433. 
We qrish to thank you and your staff for the
work in providing that assistance.
while it eras very helpful , it does not dispose of all the
issues. In fact, it raised a few additionaL issues which are
the subject of this request for further inforroal assistance.
The prior assistance request in effect assuned that alL of the
work would be perforrned by registered civil 
engineers in
responsible charge of the various design work or benefit
spread formula work who are principals in the consulting
firms. 
No distinction was nade as to the levet of ownership
of the various engineers.
Assume, for a moment, that the other e],ements of Government
Code section 87100 are met, namely the trpublic officialrt 
and
the 'rparticipates in" elenents, and that all of the work is
performed under the responsible charge of a registered civil
engineer who does not own a 108 interest in the consutting
engineering firro. 
Does that avoicl the application of the
conflict of interest provisions? Assume further that the
consulting engineering finn received $250 or nore in the
12 nonths prior to the decision, and that there is no material
financial effect on the consulting f irrn.
Your infornal assistance response dated April l-2, L99o, in
footnote 5 on page 5, discusses the application of Goverrunent
Code section 82030 and apparently concludes that if 
the
engineer has less than a 10t os/nership interest in a firm, the

disgual i fication obligations would apply only if the decision
had a material financial effect on the firn, rather than
clients of the firn. 
while we agree with that concLusion, it
certainly creates an anomalous situation.
Large firns that have many qualified registered civil
engineers or licensed land surveyors who may not own any
interest in the firn or, alternatively, who own less than a
l-08 interest, could avoid the d1sq,:a1if ication prcvisicns; but
smaller firns, where the only engineers who are appropriately
registered al.so are greater-than-lo8 owners, would be subject
to then. In effect, it discriminates against smaller firms
in performing such work.
Your informal assistance letter of April L2, LggO, on page 9,
third paragraph from the top, concludes that if 
a
subconsultant provides the desigrn for the improvements and
prepares the estinates for the inprovements, he participates
in the making of a governmental decision. Frankl.y, from a
corDmon-sense perspective, it is the preparation of the benefit
spread forrnula that provides the obvious opportunity to favor
one client over a non-cl,ient. It really is difficult for the
engineering connunity to understand how the preparation of
various design servj-ces is likely to benefit a client to the
exclusion of a non-client.
In the prior request for infornal assistance, we did not go
into great detail regarding the plan check requirenent which
exists with respect to virtuaLl.y alt engineering design work.
When an engineer prepares plans and specifications, they are
reviewed by the plan check departnent of the public agencyi
and, frankly, they frequently are very critically 
reviewed.
!,'hy does tiie plan check requirement not satisfy t,he
f'signifi.cant substantive reviewrr requirements of l-B7oO (c) (2)?
Similarly, the preparation of a boundary map for recordation
as part of the work for a benefit assessnent district is going
to be subject to review prior to recordation. Siurilarly, the
assessment diagrams, which show the ownership of the various
individual parcels within the boundary of the proposed
assessment district, will be subject to review and approval .
In fact, even the construction cost estinates and the district
cost estirnates are very likely to be scrutinized and reviewed
by the public agency. Another function performed by the
engineering firn is the posting and nailinq of notices, as the
benefit assessnent district approaches the public hearing
stage of the decision-naking process.


Kathryn E. Donovan, ceneral counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMITISSION
June 21, 1990
Page 3
Frankly, it 
seems to us that so long as there is an
independent plan check or review, there is no "participation
intr the naking of a governmental decision pursuant to the
cited regulation.
Alternatively, if the engineer structures his relationship,
contractually, with the public agency so that he operates as
a true independen+- contrac'r-or and expressly adcpts both of ',-he
provisions of 18700(a) (2) (A) and (B), it seens that the
engineer can avoid being a trconsul"tantrr and, hence, avoid
being a i'public of f iciat. rl
Would you please comment on the ttparticipates inrr regulation
as discussed above, and also whether or not the consul-tant can
contractually avoid being a rrconsultanttr if he includes in his
contract both of the cited regnrlatory provisions.
Sone engineering firrns provide assessment district consulting
services and never have any fornal contract with the public
agency. If there is no oral or written contract with the
public agency, rather aII the work is done for the private
developer and paid for by the private developer, does that
avoid the application of the conflict of j-nterest rules?
While the ability to favor a ctient over a non-client sti11
exists, it seems that technically that enqineer or engineerj-ng
firn would not be a trconsuLtantrr and, hence, not a ttpublic
official'r absent a contract with the public agency.
Again, Iet me enphasize our appreciation of the work that has
been done in rendering infonnal assistance at our request.
We also specifically r.rould like to thank Jeevan Ahuj a for
appearing at the seurinars that recently have been conducied
by ny cl ient.
As you are aware, there is a great deal of interest in this
arnong the engineering community. white it technically has
been a problen since 1,974, it largely was dorrnant until a
recent informal opinion of your office precipitated our
involvement. It substantially inpacts the qray engineers are
accustorned to doing business; and r,rhile ny client is rnaking
efforts to educate the engineering and surveying community
regarding the implications of the Fair Political Practices
Act, we also lrould like to continue to explore ways of
avoiding the problen.


Kathryn E. Donovan, GeneraL CounseL
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
June 21, 1990
Page 4
Thank you in advance for your continuing assistance.
Very truly yours,

sections.question_synthetic: The letter raises multiple follow-up questions regarding the application of Government Code section 87100 conflict of interest provisions to civil engineers and engineering firms performing assessment district work, including whether engineers with less than 10% ownership can avoid disqualification, whether independent plan check requirements satisfy the 'significant substantive review' standard, whether contractual provisions can avoid consultant/public official status, and whether lack of a formal contract with the public agency eliminates conflict of interest obligations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from a law firm seeking FPPC guidance on conflict of interest issues, not an FPPC response. The document references a prior April 12, 1990 advice letter (File No. I-89-433) but that prior response is not included here. The current letter raises several distinct legal questions but does not follow a formal Q&A structure. Document is dated June 21, 1990.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The letter raises multiple follow-up questions regarding the application of Government Code section 87100 conflict of interest provisions to civil engineers and engineering firms performing assessment district work, including whether engineers with less than 10% ownership can avoid disqualification, whether independent plan check requirements satisfy the 'significant substantive review' standard, whether contractual provisions can avoid consultant/public official status, and whether lack of a formal contract with the public agency eliminates conflict of interest obligations.

embedding.summary: On behalf of the California Council of Civil Engineers & Land Surveyors, the law firm Turner & Sullivan submits an informal assistance request to the FPPC raising multiple follow-up questions about the application of conflict of interest rules to engineering firms performing assessment district consulting work, building on a previous advice letter.
